segment-text: Pathe Productions Ltd 6 Ramillies Street
Calamity Films Portobello Studios 138 Portobello Road
EXT. MARK'S BLOCK OF COUNCIL FLATS. EARLY MORNING.
A stack of drab council flats. Only one stands out, a huge hand painted banner, hanging from its window. THATCHER OUT.
INT. MARK'S FLAT. KITCHEN. DAY.

A CLASH poster above the cooker, a Soviet flag beside it partially obscured by a life size cut out of Eartha Kitt. MARK walks in, wearing boxer shorts, and switches on a crappy little portable TV. TISWAS. Lenny Henry being gunged in a cage. He switches over. A wave of policemen charging a picket line of striking miners. He switches on the kettle...
With the strike now entering its fourth month, neither the miners

nor the police ...opens a kitchen cupboard and pulls down a mug. His hand hovers for a moment over a second, before deciding against it. MARK'S attention flits to the set - a MINER'S WIFE with an infant on her hip.
...Trying to feed our kids. We're being bullied by the police. Lied
about in the papers A tea-bag dumped into the mug. MARGARET THATCHER is on now REPORTER (V.O.) - by the Prime Minister last night. MARGARET THATCHER (ON TV)
MARK'S expression changes. A YOUNG GUY enters, buttoning his shirt. The kettle starts to boil.
I left my number. Just in case.
By their actions, we have seen a new birth of leadership in Britain.
Maybe see you on the march, then.
MARK'S oblivious. The YOUNG GUY leaves. The kettle's reaching a crescendo of steaming and bubbling.
And that is the most important thing - the most enduring thing that is going to come out of this coal strike. A new birth of leadership!
The kettle clicks. Done. MARK starts to smile.
INT. MARK'S FLAT. KITCHEN. DAY.

MARGARET THATCHER still speaking. MARK, frantically rummaging in the cupboards. He finds what he's been looking for. A huge PLASTIC BUCKET is triumphantly plonked down in front of the TV obscuring everything else completely. We marvel at it, filling the screen. Then the bucket is snatched up and we're whisked off with it to (OVER CREDITS:)
EXT. COUNCIL BLOCK. DAY.
MARK now has a few buckets beside him and his neighbours are handing him more. An OLD MAN shouts at MARK from above OLD MAN
There's no need to do that, just knock on the door, we'd let you in.
The WOMEN laugh. The OLD MAN gets apoplectic.
(Shouting after) They're sending a policeman.
(Turning, waving) Oh, I do hope so.
He heads off, barely balancing his armfuls of buckets.
INT. JOE'S PARENT'S HOUSE LIVING ROOM. DAY. Bromley. A comfortable, suburban living room: JOE (20) opens

a birthday present over breakfast with his MUM and DAD. JOE'S DAD is watching the Miners' Strike on the news with rapt attention. JOE opens the present to reveal a brand new camera. There's a card with a badge attached: 20 TODAY! JOE smiles at his MUM then turns to his DAD.
Thanks, Dad.
JOE'S DAD nods but doesn't take his eyes off the TV. JOE takes a sneaky look at the clock on the mantelpiece.
INT. STEPH'S BEDROOM. DAY.

STEPH; (25) plonks herself down in front of a mirror covered in post-cards and cut outs of forties screen icons. She sticks a cigarette into the corner of her mouth, eyes the pictures STEPH Right then, ladies - and starts pumping her mascara.
EXT. ALLOTMENT. DAY.
An allotment lock-up. MIKE (28) is standing over a sheet on the floor with a large dripping paint-brush in his hand. He starts to paint.
INT. JONATHAN AND GETHIN'S BEDROOM. DAY.
GETHIN (Late 30'S) walks into his bedroom and looks at his boyfriend, JONATHAN (Late 30'S) cocooned under the duvet. He looks at his watch, then at the lump.
(Smiley) You coming? GETHIN has a slight Welsh lilt, smoothed out by years of
living in London. The lump grunts - and none too kindly then rolls over. GETHIN shakes his head.
EXT. STREET. DAY.
JOE running to catch a suburban train.
EXT. STREET. DAY. A shot of the London skyline. Beginning of a June day. EXT. STREET. DAY.
MARK, loaded with buckets, marching past a wall plastered with graffiti and dominated with a huge anarchy symbol.
EXT. TUBE STATION. DAY.
JOE comes to an astonished standstill. Caption on screen:
Saturday June 30th, 1984. GAY MEN and WOMEN marching past the

end of the street. Banners, whistles, all very political. It's edgy. Defiant. JOE watches as a banner sails past: GAY LIBERATION FRONT. GETHIN is at the forefront of this group, he's blowing on a whistle, his fist raised in the air. The caption continues GAY PRIDE MARCH. LONDON. JOE does a little controlled breathing to soothe his nerves.
EXT. GAY PRIDE PROTEST MARCH. DAY.
JOE trots alongside the march. One foot on the kerb. One foot off. A gang of skinheads are shouting abuse. One flings a can at him. A policeman laughs. JOE ducks into the safety of the march.
EXT. GAY PRIDE PROTEST MARCH. DAY.

JOE arrives in the march. He's walking beside MIKE still struggling with his rolled up banner. They walk side by side, JOE still looking for his nearest available exit. Skinheads are on both sides doing Nazi salutes.
Here, mate - grab hold of this will you? (The banner)
Oh. Sorry. I'm not MIKE Five minutes. Just til my friends get here.
It's just - I don't really want to be too visible.
The banner unfurls: QUEERS! BETTER BLATANT THAN LATENT.
First Pride?
First anything.
This is the best way. Throw yourself in.
The thing is - I'm actually from Bromley.
Don't worry about that. We're a broad church.
No, I mean - sorry - it's the train. It's a bit further out than people think MIKE (Shouted, crossly) Oi! - I've been dragging this on my
own since Marble Arch STEPH has appeared. JEFF following; both carrying buckets.
Are they buckets?
I'm surprised you have to ask that question, Mike, coming from Accrington.
Mike, does anything get rid of love bites?
No. Where is Mark now?
Right. Listen up everyone MARK'S arrived. JOE is immediately struck by his energy.
Where exactly have you been?
I want everyone to take a bucket
and start rattling. This is for the
We agreed on a banner.
It's a show of solidarity. Who hates the miners? Thatcher. Who else? The police, the public and the tabloid press. Sound familiar?
But surely MARK The only problem we've got that they haven't is Mary Whitehouse. And that can only be a matter of time. Mark -
I know. It's not been planned, it's not been thought through - but it's a really good idea isn't it? (Big smile) Isn't it?
And what am I supposed to do with this?
Give it to the Lesbians. They love a banner.
Looks like you're off the hook, mate.

JOE watches as the banner is passed back to a bunch of lesbians behind. Released from its grip, he suddenly feels very exposed. He tries to walk alongside this new group for a moment, but as they disperse to collect cash, he falters and steps away.
EXT. DAY. GAY PRIDE PROTEST MARCH.

JOE stands, watching. A WOMAN approaches, with a LITTLE GIRL. She sees the march then turns to JOE. WOMAN WITH LITTLE GIRL (Gestures to the march) Disgusting. JOE smiles and nods. The WOMAN and LITTLE GIRL move on. JOE looks sickened and unhappy. He's about to turn away when he hears MARK'S VOICE (O.S.)
miners and their families. JOE plunges himself back in. He pushes through the crowd, searching wildly. Finally he spots MIKE, rattling his bucket.
I was wondering - Do you need-?
Well, well. It's Bromley.
I mean, my last train's actually

not for ages MIKE hands him a bucket. He smiles and starts rattling. Just as he starts to get into his stride he stops and sees a SWEET LITTLE OLD LADY is standing beside the march holding up a lovingly home-made sign. It reads BURN IN HELL.
EXT. GAY'S THE WORD BOOKSHOP. NIGHT.
A post Pride party in full swing. The building throbs with music, people hang out of every window. We find a phone box outside...
INT. PHONE-BOX / TONY AND MARION'S HOUSE EVENING.
JOE on the phone. Echoing, stinky London phone box. MARION at home with Larry Grayson on TV behind.
It's just a couple of lads from College. And one of them lives in Chiselhurst so he can't be late either. I've got my key.
Just mind yourself on that last train. There's weirdos and all sorts on there.
Thanks Mum.
Joe puts the phone down, pushes open the door and hurries into...
INT. HALLWAY. GETHIN AND JONATHAN'S FLAT ABOVE G.T.W. NIGHT.23

JOE climbing the narrow stairs to the flat above the shop. It's an explosion of noise and life. New Romantics, Bronski Beat 'flat-tops', the odd Mohican. There are banners hanging from the bannisters, torn and trampled like battle pennants. A handsome, shirtless punk passes, hands him a beer. JOE blushes. He sees MARK and MIKE through the open door to a bedroom, counting coins. He stares at MARK for a second. GIRL'S VOICE (O.S.) Oi Bromley. (He looks around) Down here. STEPH, sitting, smoking. He goes to her.
I'm hiding from that girl. Why?
She broke my heart at a Smith's concert.
I've never met a lesbian before.
Really? I've never met anyone who irons their jeans.
I live at home.
No shit. (Points to his badge) And is that where you got that lovely brooch?
That's embarrassing.
It's also illegal, darling. Sixteen for breeders. Twenty-one for gays. Did you learn nothing on that march? You're still a minor. Jesus -
JOE removes it hurriedly. STEPH takes his can, lifts it.

Victory to the minors. What about Mum and Dad? Do they know you're a big fairy? (JOE shakes his head) I'd keep it that way if I were you. My mum threw me out when I was fifteen. Mind you, I was snogging the baby-sitter.
MARK, MIKE and several others, troop past them. JEFF appears.
They've counted it.
(Turning) Well, come on. You collected as much as anyone.
JOE chases after her, joining the column now walking downstairs. A man appears beside JEFF, CHARLIE.
(Very spiky) Stephanie, were you with Jeff in The Bell last night?
(Touching his neck) Charlie, for fuck's sake - it's a bruise okay? My neck. Is bruised.
JOE passes them, following STEPH into the shop downstairs.
INT. GAY'S THE WORD BOOKSHOP. EVENING.
The shop has about twenty people in it. (Including RAY and REGGIE(20'S) GETHIN and CHARLIE.) MARK and MIKE are sitting next to each other. The party thumps away upstairs.
Pretty good march today. Not much in the way of beatings or abuse. Hardly any petrol bombs or Swastikas. Is it me? Or are the Police getting soft?
A small laugh. MARK'S in his element. Just warming up.

It's funny - They've stopped hanging around outside our clubs lately? What's that about? Do you think they finally got sick of all that Donna Summer? (Bigger laugh) My guess is they went somewhere else. To pick on someone else. My guess is that while we're enjoying
a temporary reprieve, they're here MARK holds up A TABLOID. A POLICEMAN squaring up to a MINER.
- Giving these poor sods the shit we usually get. Now these mining communities are being bullied. Just like we are. Bullied by the police. Bullied by the tabloids. Bullied by the government.
Do any of them need a hug?
No. What they need is cash. And they need it urgently.
Yeah, because the miners have always come to our aid, haven't they?
Why don't we talk about today?
Today. With only a couple of buckets. We raised nearly two hundred quid. Think what we could achieve if we really started trying.
I'm from Durham.
Well, you know what we're talking about then.
I know those bastards kicked the shit out of me every morning on my way to school. And every night on my way home.
A chill little pause. GETHIN watches him as he leaves.
We're proposing to meet at least once a week - And to do as many collections as we can MARK Oh, and we've got a name.
MARK turns the bucket round to reveal initials drawn on.
LGSM. Lesbians. And Gays. Support. The Miners.
It's not very catchy.
It's a support group, Steph. Not a Skiffle band.
Let's have a show of hands, shall we? Who's in?
MARK and MIKE raise their hands. STEPH too. JEFF, RAY,
Is that it?
JOE hangs back. By now the room is emptying.
Come on guys. Please.
MIKE, MARK, STEPH and JEFF are joined by RAY and REGGIE.
We've actually been looking for things we can do together. As a couple.
This is perfect. You can feed the miners and your relationship. How many's that?
(Unimpressed)
That's better than five.
And not as good as seven.
GETHIN steps forward.
(Under his breath) Oh God. Here come the Gay Libbers.
MARK administers a swift kick.
Brilliant party, Geth.
I'm sure you can use the back room here - if you're looking for a base, that is MARK (Pointedly at JEFF) We are. That's amazing Gethin. Thank you.
And what about Jonathan? (Glance to JEFF) Or is it exclusively for the under twenty-fives?
Of course not. Everyone's welcome MIKE
Are you sure Jonathan's interested? JONATHAN is visible through the shop window, drunk, dancing.
He's at a bit of a loose end at the moment. He just needs something to occupy him. A project.
What about Bromley over there?
They all look at JOE.
Well. I do have - I've just started
Good. Congratulations all of you. You are the founder members of Lesbians and Gays Support the Miners.
(Dryly) Terrific. Let's bring down the government.
EXT. GAY'S THE WORD BOOKSHOP. DAY.
RAY and REGGIE, STEPH and JEFF in two sets of pairs, fervently rattling buckets outside the shop.
INT. BROMLEY CATERING COLLEGE. DAY.
JOE at CATERING COLLEGE, staring into space.
And there's the desired
consistency. Glossy and wobbly. The LECTURER shakes the glass bowl. JOE doesn't look up. LECTURER (CONT'D) Glossy and wobbly.
INT. STOCK-ROOM. GAY'S THE WORD BOOKSHOP. DAY
MIKE and MARK in the stockroom on the phone. VOICE ON PHONE
(Into the phone) Hello, I represent a group called Lesbians and Gays support the
miners He stops. Looks at MARK. The phone's been put down on him.
INT. GAY'S THE WORD BOOKSHOP. DAY.
GAY'S THE WORD BOOKSHOP. JOE and STEPH, collecting buckets. GETHIN is re-stocking the shelves. He spots a rather hungover looking JONATHAN and goes to the door.
(Calling) Why don't you go out with them today?
Because I have better things to do than run around with a bunch of kids.
(Shouted after) What happened to Gay Lib, Jonathan?
(Looking at the 'kids') I don't know. What did happen to it?
He goes. JOE and STEPH are watching from the back.
Is that Gethin's boyfriend?
Jonathan. He stabbed Susannah York with an ice pick. (JOE looks at her) He's an actor.
JOE watches JONATHAN walk away.
INT. GAY'S THE WORD BOOKSHOP. DAY.
MARK on the phone now...
(Into the phone) We've raised some money, and we're looking for a mining community to (listens) Lesbians and Gays support the miners - Sure, but you've said
that before Another phone down. MARK looks at MIKE.
They'll call back. They will.
INT. STOCK-ROOM. GAY'S THE WORD BOOKSHOP. DAY.
Straight after. LGSM meet. MIKE and MARK at loggerheads.
Mark - Face it.
They're on strike. They're busy.
They don't want to take our money because we're poofs.
(Quietly) And a Dyke.
They'd rather starve.
Maybe we should just hand the money over. Anonymously. I mean, we don't have to say we're gay do we ? No.
At least then, we'd be helping.
No. This is a gay and lesbian group and we are unapologetic about that. Jesus, why are we even wasting time with all this?
MARK grabs a bucket and sets off. He stops at the door. Mark -
They will call us back - okay? It has nothing to do with the fact that we're Poofs STEPH And a Dyke.
They will call us back. End of meeting.
He goes.
They're never going to call us back.
Somebody needs to go with him.
Nobody moves.
First rule of the group - ? I'll go.
Thank you, Steph.
STEPH leaves. MIKE shakes his head and looks at the others.
EXT. STREET. DAY.
MARK is banging the hell out of his bucket.
Lesbians and Gays support the miners!
A PASSER-BY spits on the pavement in front of MARK. PASSER-BY
STEPH arrives.
First rule of the group, Comrade. Nobody collects alone. (Rattles her bucket) Lesbians and Gays support the miners!
MARK stops rattling his bucket and stares into space for a moment. Suddenly he turns and marches away, leaving STEPH.
Lesbians and gays - (He's gone) I don't believe this She runs after him.
INT. STOCK-ROOM. GAY'S THE WORD BOOKSHOP. DAY.
GETHIN is rooting through drawers. Everyone gathered around.
Come on, Gethin. You're supposed to be a bookshop.
We don't have maps. It's a gay bookshop. People ask for the poems of Walt Whitman.
STEPH enters, plonks her bucket down, angrily.
I hope there's a good reason why I've been abandoned in the street?
(Withering sarcasm) We're going to pick some mining town completely at random - and, "just ring it." That's the plan, is it, Mark?
Yep. The town hall, the council -
Easy as that.
Why not? Bypass the union all together.
I think it's inspired. So do I.
Here we go...
See? Even Bromley agrees. Aha!
GETHIN has pulled out a dusty AA road atlas. JEFF snatches it, rifles through.
What do we want? Somewhere North. Industrial.
Humberside. Is that a place?
(Snatching it back) Jesus Christ GETHIN rifles through the pages.
If it's miners you're looking for Wales. He drops the atlas on the table, open at South Wales. Of course.
Everyone crowds in.
(Pointing at the map) That is a fucking big coal field.
That's the Brecon Beacons. But that's a coalfield. And that is.
And what are we supposed to do? Just stick a pin in it?
Do you know people, Gethin?
I haven't been back there in sixteen years. Why not?
He shakes his head. A beat.
Let's just say there isn't always a welcome in the hillsides. Shall I get the phonebook?
MIKE looks at MARK. He's thinking. Mark's eyes are alive with excitement. Impatient for MIKE'S approval. Well?
What's the worst that can happen?
Hello. I represent a bunch of
screaming homosexuals Laughter. GETHIN leaves.
May I inquire about your communal baths?
What's that got to do with the strike?
Nothing. I'd just like to enquire.

Laughter. GETHIN plonks the phone down on the table. He has a phone book with him, which he starts to rifle through. MARK reaches for the phone, enjoying the laughter. MIKE pulls it back and puts it squarely in front of himself.
(Over the laughter) Alright. Thank you. If we're going to do this, we need to take it seriously.
Silence. MIKE looks at MARK. MARK'S intense gaze on him. MIKE'S expression gives nothing away.
Now what's the Welsh for Lesbian?
More laughs. MARK grins. MIKE smiles as he picks up the receiver.
INT. ONLLWYN MINER'S WELFARE HALL. DAY.

Large and deserted Miners social club. Sun streaming in from a high window. Dust floating in the sunlight. A telephone rings in the foreground. It rings and rings. Finally, a small, sweet, frail OLD LADY appears far away, holding a teacup and a tea-towel. She stands, looking at the ringing phone for a moment. She looks off towards the kitchen and then back at the phone. Still ringing. Finally, she walks slowly over to the phone. She puts down the cup and the tea towel and lifts the receiver. As she does so, a TITLE appears on the screen Miners Welfare Hall, Onllwyn, Dulais Valley, South Wales. Her voice is sweet, melodious, gentle. Hello - ?
She listens for a moment. We can only hear indistinct, muffled speech on the other end. She nods. Yes - ?
GWEN (CONT'D)

More talking from the other end. The camera moves closer and closer to GWEN'S face as she listens to what's being said. Slowly we watch the dawning realisation. Her little eyes widen. Finally, and with a certain amount of polite but discernible strain, she manages a high pitched sentence. I see.
INT. STOCK-ROOM GAY'S THE WORD BOOKSHOP. DAY.
LGSM celebrate their victory. Hugging & Cheering.
EXT. CAFE. LATE AFTERNOON. NEXT DAY.
STEPH, JOE, JEFF, MIKE, and MARK waiting anxiously outside.
Welsh people have really black hair, don't they? Or Ginger.
It really didn't occur to you to get a description?
She was off the phone that fast JEFF Maybe we should try and look more obviously gay.
Achievable goals please, Jeff.
A few more people pass by. They continue to scan the street.
I used to date this boy from Cardiff and he had really white blonde hair.
Ash.
No. White. Dead white. Shut up Flaxen.
Shut up. That's him.
They follow MARK'S gaze. DAI DONOVAN (40'S) has stopped nearby and appears to be scrutinizing them.
Are you sure?
He doesn't have black hair. Or Ginger.
Oh my god, he's coming towards us.
They watch. MARK raises himself up, smiles. DAI nods.
How did you - ?
Same as in a nightclub. It's all in
the eyes. DAI reaches them. Smiles broadly. Everyone
I'm Dai Donovan. I'm from the Dulais Valley. You must be - ?
Yes. Hello.
Hand shaking, greeting from everyone, 'hello' etc. DAI takes them in for a second. There's a smiley, awkward silence.
So. LGSM. What does that stand for, then?
INT. CAFE. EARLY EVENING.
DAI is looking slightly shell shocked sitting with LGSM, at a very cramped table. The BAG is on the table between them.
never dreamed for a moment it was L for He gestures lightly at STEPH.
And this money you've collected. That's all from - gays and lesbians? Mostly.
Well. There we are. 'From whence cometh my help?'
This is just the beginning. Oh?
We've got big plans. Well, there's no point pretending I'm not surprised. You can see that. Truth told you're the first gays I've ever met in my life.
(Friendly) As far as you're aware. That's true.
And you're the first miner I've ever met. Everyone acknowledges this - 'Me too, yeah etc.'
Now I want you to do something for me. I want you to go back to your community and I want you to convey my thanks - my personal thanks and the thanks of all the people of
Of course we will. Won't we?
Nodding, 'Yes.' 'Absolutely.' All except MARK. He's staring.
Won't we, Mark?
'I lift up mine eyes unto the hills. And from whence cometh my help?'
Well spotted.
I was an altar boy (Huge grin) Who fancies a pint?
INT. BACKSTAGE. THE BELL, GAY PUB. EVENING.
Dingy corridor. The roar of the punters, loud music. A DRAG QUEEN leads DAI, MARK, MIKE, JOE, STEPH and JEFF backstage.
You can have five minutes.
Are you sure about this? Dead sure.
Yeah, but for Dai DAI I'm alright, lad.
Trust me, Dai - if you can handle this - it's going to make a huge difference.
What's he going to do? Take his clothes off?
I'm going to say thank you.
Well, don't blame me if you get bottled.

He pulls back a tatty little streamer curtain and walks out onto the stage. MARK follows him. We hear rather a lot of cheers/boos/general rowdiness. Then on the Mic DRAG QUEEN (O.S.) (CONT'D) Right. Shut up you fuckers MARK'S VOICE (O.S.) Thank you. Right. Listen. Some of you know me - My name is Mark
VOICE (O.S.)
I'm going to invite somebody onto the stage now who wants to talk to
you A few boos. JOE looks over at DAI. He looks nervous.
something he wants to say to you MARK comes over and pulls back the streamers. DAI steps out. JOE, STEPH, MIKE and JEFF look at each other. Come on -
STEPH leads them, rushing round to the front to watch.
INT. THE BELL, GAY PUB. EVENING.
DAI walks out onto the stage. There's a bit of Mic feedback and he looks out at the crowd. Expectant faces looking up at him. The DRAG QUEEN and MARK are standing on each side.
Right then. I've had a lot of new experiences during this strike.
Speaking in public. Standing on a
(Shouted) If you don't like it go home!
GETHIN admonishes him with a dig in the ribs.

As a matter of fact I do like it. Beer's a bit expensive, mind. (A cheer). But really - there's only one difference between this and a bar in South Wales. The women. (Looks at DRAG QUEEN) They're a lot more feminine in here.
Even bigger cheer. JONATHAN roars with laughter.
But what I'm here to say to you tonight - and don't worry, it won't take long - is thank you. Silence. They're really listening. This is unexpected. MARK
looks out into the crowd. JONATHAN is listening intently too.
If you're one of the people who's put money into these buckets - if you've supported LGSM - thank you.
GETHIN looks at JONATHAN. He's listening. GETHIN smiles.

Because what you've given us is more than money. It's friendship. And when you're in a fight as bitter and as important as this one, against an enemy, so much bigger, so much stronger than you well. To find out that you have a friend you never knew existed It's the best thing in the world.
INT. THE BELL, GAY PUB. EVENING.
Money pouring into the buckets now. Crowds around DAI DONOVAN. STEPH talks to two slightly earnest Lesbians, ZOE and STELLA who are dropping money into her bucket.
So you're the only girl?
That's right. I'm the L in LGSM.
MARK and MIKE are at the bar, JOE beside them.
That was a risky strategy.
Never be afraid of the grand
gesture, Mike.
I was more afraid of getting my head kicked in.
MARK brandishes his bucket - brimming with coins. Beams.
Looks like they're taking us seriously now though, doesn't it?
(Smiles) It does, Mark, yeah. It does.
MARK plants a kiss on him. MIKE glows. JONATHAN approaches DAI with GETHIN behind. Stretches his hand. DAI shakes it.
Good speech. I've worked a few tough crowds myself, over the years.
In politics?
In panto.
MAN'S VOICE (O.S.) Okay, everybody!
They look up. There's someone standing on the bar with a camera. Everybody squeezes into position together.
That's something else we could use. What?
An official photographer.
Jesus, Mark, we don't even have an official typewriter. MAN'S VOICE (O.S.) Nice and close, please.
I could do it. I've got a camera. A really good one. I'd be happy to do it.
Alright, Bromley. If you're sure MARK Of course he's sure. Never let it be said that LGSM discouraged Youth enterprise.
Right then JOE beams. They all squeeze in around DAI at the bar.
What are you all going to say? Cheese?
Victory to the miners!
There's a huge cheer. The flash goes off. It's an image of joy and energy. In sharp contrast to 44
EXT. DULAIS VILLAGE. DAY.

A tattered VICTORY TO THE MINERS poster slapped against a wall. Cold, wet, morning. A troop of MINERS are climbing into a PICKET BUS. SIAN JAMES and her husband MARTIN (Both 20'S, wrapped up against the rain) walking towards the village hall. SIAN looks up at it, nervously. One of the MINERS climbing into the bus, waves to MARTIN.
Martin! Come on!
He waves back, turns to SIAN. He kisses her.
Tell them you've run a whole canteen before. You're not just packing food parcels. Go.
And don't look so bloody worried. It's not the first day of school.
She pushes him away, affectionately. MARTIN runs off to the bus and climbs aboard just as it starts to back-fire noisily. SIAN faces the town hall.
INT. ONLLWYN MINER'S WELFARE HALL. DAY.
SIAN is packing food parcels, with OTHER WIVES. It's dull work. She looks across the room wistfully at the Welfare committee. The meeting's in full swing.
What do they need to come here for? We sent them a thank you note.
Every other support group has been
Every other support group was approved by the whole committee. Oh, I see.
Dai had to make a snap decision, Maureen.
This is all my fault.
He was in London - What would you have done? Told them to keep their money?
Alright - I run a civil meeting here. If you want to scream and shout, get on the pickets.
That exchange was between MAUREEN ROPER (40'S) DAI and his
wife MARGARET DONOVAN (40'S), GWEN (70'S) the old lady who
answered the phone to LGSM and the Queen Bee of the miners' wives, HEFINA HEDDON(50'S) a fiery, powerful woman, who has run the Miners Welfare for many years. SIAN is listening intently, fascinated.
I don't have a problem with - what they are.
None of us do.
